title: Chapter 48: The Final Price
content: The First Seat stood before the Hell Gate, its form flickering between solid and void.
"You have no card," it said. "No wealth. No contract. You are a mortal girl standing before a god."
"And you're a god who's about to destroy everything because you can't handle losing a vote."
"The vote was irrelevant. The system is what matters. And you broke it."
"I fixed it. There's a difference."
"There is no difference between breaking and fixing when you are the one who decides which is which."
The First Seat raised its hand. Behind it, the Hell Gate's teeth began to move — grinding, opening.
"You think you restored the Void. But the Void was always weak. It was too soft. Too trusting. That's why it fell. And that's why you will fall too."
From the gate, something began to emerge.
Not a form. A pressure. A weight that pressed against reality itself, making the air scream.
Silas stepped forward. "That is not a Primordial. That is something older."
"The Void's parent," the First Seat said. "The chaos that existed before the first contract. The original darkness. It has no name because names did not exist when it was born."
The pressure intensified. Roxanne felt her knees buckle.
But she did not fall.
She straightened her spine.
"You think I need a card to win?"
"You do. You always did."
"The card was never my power. It was my tool. The power was always the Void. And the Void doesn't need a card to assert itself."
She stepped forward. Toward the gate. Toward the emerging chaos.
"I am the heir of the Primordial Void. That means I am the living contract. I don't need to swipe. I don't need to spend. I am the transaction."
She raised her empty hand.
"And I say this chaos has no standing in my system."
The pressure stopped.
The emerging entity paused.
And the First Seat's eyes went wide.
"You cannot —"
"I just did."
She looked at the chaos before her. It had no face, but she spoke to it anyway.

"You predate the contract. But the contract is the foundation of the current reality. Without it, you have no form to take, no existence to claim, no ground to stand on. You are a concept without a host."
The chaos pressed against her words. But it could not advance.
"Return to the dark. Wait for a system that recognizes you. This one doesn't anymore."
The entity paused.
Then, slowly, it began to retreat.
The First Seat screamed.
Not a sound — a conceptual scream. A violation of the rules it had spent forty thousand years upholding.
"You cannot command the primordial dark!"
"I'm not commanding it. I'm denying it entry. There's a difference."
The Hell Gate's teeth stopped grinding.
The chaos receded.
The gate began to close.
And the First Seat stood alone.
